questions.q: According to social contract theory, legitimate government authority derives from
options: Divine right granted by religious institutions
The consent of the governed who agree to be bound by laws and institutions
Conquest by military force that cannot be challenged
Tradition and hereditary succession alone
questions.explanation: Social contract theory, developed by Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau, holds that political authority gains legitimacy through agreement by those governed, not divine right, force, or mere tradition. The people voluntarily surrender some freedoms for security and order.

questions.q: In Hobbes's Leviathan, life in the state of nature is characterized as
options: Peaceful and cooperative without government interference
Solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short
Guided by natural moral law that requires no enforcement
Regulated by rational self-interest without conflict
questions.explanation: Hobbes famously described natural life as a war of all against all without sovereign authority. Reasonable people therefore consent to a powerful ruler (Leviathan) to maintain order. This pessimistic view contrasts with those who see natural law or cooperation as natural.

questions.q: Locke's conception of natural rights includes all of the following EXCEPT
options: The right to life and liberty
The right to property acquired through labor
The right to absolute obedience to the sovereign without question
The right to pursue happiness within limits of law
questions.explanation: Locke argued for life, liberty, and property as natural rights that precede government and which government exists to protect. He explicitly rejected absolute obedience to sovereigns; limited government exists to preserve these rights, not suppress them.

questions.q: Rousseau's concept of the general will refers to
options: The desires of the monarch or ruling elite
The collective determination of what serves the common good, ideally expressed through direct democracy
Majority votes on any issue regardless of principle
The commands of philosophers who understand truth
questions.explanation: Rousseau proposed that legitimate law emerges from the general will—what the community collectively determines benefits all. This emphasizes popular sovereignty and direct participation, not elite rule, simple majorities ignoring principles, or philosopher-kings.

questions.q: The concept of natural rights theory suggests that certain rights are
options: Granted by the state and revocable at its pleasure
Inherent to human beings and exist prior to government
Created through democratic voting and majority decision
Provided only to citizens of wealthy nations
questions.explanation: Natural rights theory asserts that fundamental rights—life, liberty, property, conscience—are inherent to humanity and government's role is to protect them, not grant them. This contrasts with positivism, which sees rights as state-created.

questions.q: Montesquieu's contribution to political theory particularly emphasized
options: The absolute necessity of monarchy
The separation of powers to prevent tyranny through concentrated authority
That democracy is always superior to all other systems
The irrelevance of institutional design to political outcomes
questions.explanation: Montesquieu analyzed existing systems and argued that dividing power among branches—executive, legislative, judicial—prevents tyranny better than concentrating it. This influenced American constitutional design. He did not advocate absolute monarchy or claim all systems equivalent.

questions.q: Liberal political theory values individual liberty primarily because
options: Individuals are naturally selfish and must be controlled
Individuals are the basic unit of moral and political concern, deserving autonomy and rights
Collective welfare always supersedes individual interests
Economic efficiency requires minimal personal freedom
questions.explanation: Liberalism places individuals at the center of political concern, arguing they possess rights and deserve autonomy within a framework protecting others' equal rights. Liberals recognize collective goods exist but reject reducing all concerns to group welfare.

questions.q: Republicanism as a political theory emphasizes
options: Rule by a single individual or monarch
A system where power ultimately rests with the people and is exercised through elected representatives
Direct democracy where every citizen votes on every issue
The supremacy of religious authority in government
questions.explanation: Republicanism stresses popular sovereignty—the people are the ultimate source of authority—exercised through representatives and institutions. It is compatible with democracy but distinct from pure direct democracy or monarchy, and historically secular.

questions.q: Justice as understood in contemporary political theory includes considerations of
options: Only procedural fairness in applying existing rules
Both procedural fairness and fair distribution of resources, opportunities, and burdens
Whatever outcome maximizes the wealth of the richest citizens
Only what the strongest party demands
questions.explanation: Modern theories recognize justice involves both fair processes and outcomes—not merely procedural neutrality if distributions are wildly unequal. Theories differ on what constitutes fair distribution, but most reject pure wealth maximization for the richest as just.

questions.q: The concept of checks and balances in Federalist philosophy rests on which assumption about human nature?
options: Humans are naturally virtuous and require minimal institutional constraints
Humans tend toward power-seeking and therefore power must be divided and constrained institutionally
Virtue in leaders can be guaranteed through proper selection processes
Human nature is irrelevant to institutional design
questions.explanation: Federalist authors like Madison, influenced by historical observation, argued that humans seek power and absolute authority invites abuse. Therefore, institutions must separate and check power, making virtue in individual leaders less critical than structural safeguards.
